planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Media Downloader Bot (TikTok + X) using Node.js (ESM) and grammY

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that lets users paste public TikTok or X (Twitter) links and receive the downloadable media back in chat. The bot validates links, resolves direct media URLs via platform-specific extractors, downloads media to temporary storage, then sends it to the user as video/photo/document depending on Telegram constraints.

The system is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence (optional but recommended for rate limits, jobs, and observability). It includes robust error handling, anti-abuse measures, deduplication, a lightweight job pipeline within the process, and operational visibility (logs + basic metrics events).

Product goals:
Primary user goal: paste a public TikTok or X link and receive the media file (video/photo) in the same chat quickly and reliably.

Core product requirements:
1) Accept links in private chats and groups.
2) Support only public content. If the media is private, geo-restricted, removed, or requires authentication, the bot must explain it cannot process it.
3) Provide a simple UX: user pastes a link, bot replies with progress and then the media.
4) Be resilient to platform changes and rate limits by isolating extraction logic, adding retries/backoff, and providing graceful degradation.
5) Prevent abuse: user-level rate limiting, size limits, and basic anti-spam controls.

Non-goals (initially):
1) Downloading private media via login/cookies.
2) Complex editing/transcoding.
3) Long-term hosting of downloaded content for reuse beyond short-lived caching.

Success metrics:
1) Median time from link to delivered media.
2) Extraction success rate per platform.
3) Download/send failure rate (Telegram upload errors, file too large, timeouts).
4) Abuse incidents prevented by rate limiting.

High-level architecture:
System overview (single-process design):
1) grammY update handler receives messages and commands.
2) Link classifier identifies TikTok/X URLs and normalizes them.
3) Job manager creates an in-process download job with status tracking.
4) Extractor module resolves the public page into one or more media candidates (video variants, images, audio if applicable).
5) Downloader fetches the selected candidate to temporary storage (filesystem storage) with streaming and size guards.
6) Sender uploads to Telegram using the correct method (sendVideo/sendPhoto/sendDocument), choosing the best format/quality that fits limits.
7) Cleanup removes temporary files after send completion.

Component boundaries:
1) Bot API layer: grammY middleware for authentication checks, rate limits, and routing.
2) Domain layer: URL normalization, platform routing, job orchestration.
3) Platform adapters: TikTok adapter and X adapter. Each adapter exposes: validate(url), resolve(url) -> media candidates + metadata.
4) Media pipeline: downloader, file inspector (detect MIME/duration/size), Telegram sender, cleanup.
5) Persistence: MongoDB collections for users, jobs, and audit events (optional but recommended). In-memory maps can be used for minimal MVP, but production readiness benefits from MongoDB to survive restarts and support observability.

Runtime model:
1) Concurrency is limited with an internal queue/semaphore so the single process does not overwhelm CPU/network.
2) Each job has a state machine: queued -> resolving -> downloading -> sending -> done/failed.
3) A watchdog periodically scans for stuck jobs and marks them failed with a user-friendly message if they exceed time budgets.

External dependencies:
1) Telegram Bot API (via grammY).
2) Public web access to TikTok/X pages and media URLs.
3) MongoDB (already integrated) for persistence.
4) Temporary file storage via local filesystem storage (or object storage if later needed), but keep initial design as simple filesystem storage.

Operational considerations:
1) Structured logging for every job, including platform, timing, and failure reason.
2) Minimal health endpoint (HTTP) is optional; if present, it should only report process and MongoDB connectivity. Keep deployment generic as a Node.js server in the cloud.

Data model:
Recommended MongoDB collections (minimum production-ready):

1) users
Fields:
1) telegramUserId (number/string)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role (user/admin)
4) rateLimitState (rolling counters, lastResetAt) or store derived usage events elsewhere
5) settings (optional): preferred quality, caption on/off

2) jobs
Fields:
1) jobId (string)
2) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
3) platform (tiktok/x)
4) sourceUrlOriginal, sourceUrlNormalized
5) status (queued/resolving/downloading/sending/done/failed)
6) media
   1) type (video/photo/gallery)
   2) title/author (best-effort)
   3) candidates (array of { url, qualityLabel, mimeType, approxSize })
   4) selectedCandidate
7) file
   1) tempPath (filesystem storage path)
   2) sizeBytes
   3) mimeType
   4) checksum (optional)
8) telemetry
   1) timings: resolveMs, downloadMs, sendMs, totalMs
   2) errorCode, errorMessageSafe
9) createdAt, updatedAt, expiresAt (for cleanup policies)

3) events (optional but useful)
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) type (jobcreated, resolvefailed, ratelimited, sendfailed, etc.)
3) jobId, telegramUserId
4) payload (small, sanitized)

In-memory structures (still needed even with MongoDB):
1) Active job map: jobId -> runtime state (abort controller, progress, file stream handles).
2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.

Key flows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot interactions.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for users/jobs/events and to survive restarts.
2) In-memory maps for active jobs, locks, and short-lived caches.

Storage:
1) Simple filesystem storage for temporary downloaded media files.
2) Optional: object storage for temporary files if the filesystem is ephemeral, but keep the logic abstracted so it can be swapped without changing core flows.

Networking:
1) HTTP client for fetching public web pages and media URLs (choose one consistent client; keep timeouts/retries centralized).

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single process.
2) Configuration via environment variables (including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN).

Observability:
1) Structured logs (JSON-style) written to stdout.
2) Basic in-process metrics counters (jobs created/succeeded/failed, per-platform failures) emitted as log events.
3) Optional lightweight health check endpoint for uptime monitoring.

No additional databases or infrastructure are introduced beyond MongoDB and the single Node.js process constraints.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Concurrency limits: cap global concurrent downloads and per-platform concurrent resolves to prevent memory/network exhaustion.
2) Timeouts: strict timeouts for resolve, download, and send phases.
3) Retries with backoff: limited retries for transient network errors; no infinite retries.
4) Fallbacks: alternate media candidates when the preferred one fails.
5) Restart safety: store job status in MongoDB so you can diagnose failures after restarts; on startup, mark in-flight jobs as failed with a retriable message.

Performance:
1) Stream downloads to disk; avoid buffering large files in memory.
2) Avoid unnecessary re-resolves by caching normalized URL metadata briefly.
3) Use a single progress message edit rather than multiple messages.

Security:
1) URL allowlist and strict validation to prevent SSRF-like behavior.
2) Limit maximum download size and reject overly large media early when possible.
3) Sanitize filenames and never trust remote headers blindly.
4) Rate limiting per user and per chat to reduce abuse.
5) Admin controls for blocking users/chats.

Privacy:
1) Store only the minimum user data needed (Telegram IDs and timestamps).
2) Do not store downloaded media long term.
3) Provide a /privacy command explaining what data is stored and for how long.

Maintainability:
1) Platform adapters isolated behind a common interface so TikTok/X changes are localized.
2) Central error taxonomy (e.g., PRIVATECONTENT, NOTFOUND, RATELIMITEDUPSTREAM, TELEGRAMFILETOOLARGE) for consistent user messaging and metrics.
3) Configuration via environment variables (limits, timeouts, feature flags).

User experience:
1) Clear status updates and actionable errors.
2) If unsupported link: respond with supported platforms and examples.
3) If multiple links: either process the first or ask the user to send one at a time (configurable).

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it by sending a TikTok or X link. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show usage instructions, supported links, and common troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [General]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job logs) and retention/cleanup behavior. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /limits — Show current rate limits and maximum supported file sizes/timeouts. (usage: /limits) [General]
• /status — Show bot health summary and queue length (admin-only). (usage: /status) [Admin]
• /block — Block a user from using the bot (admin-only). (usage: /block <telegramUserId>) [Admin]
• /unblock — Unblock a previously blocked user (admin-only). (usage: /unblock <telegramUserId>) [Admin]

DOCS.md.content:
This bot downloads public media from TikTok and X links pasted into Telegram and sends the media back into the same chat.

Setup
1) Create a Telegram bot with BotFather and get your token.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.
3) Install dependencies and run:
   npm run install:root
   npm run dev

Commands
1) /start
Sends a welcome message and examples of TikTok and X URLs you can paste.

2) /help
Explains supported sources (TikTok and X), public-only limitations, common failure reasons, and privacy notes.

How it works
1) Paste a public TikTok or X link into chat.
2) The bot replies with a status message and updates it as it works (resolving, downloading, uploading).
3) The bot sends back the best available video or images.

Limitations
1) Public content only. Private, locked, age-restricted, geo-blocked, deleted content, or content requiring login cannot be downloaded.
2) Telegram upload limits apply. If a file is too large, Telegram may reject it. The bot will try fallbacks (video to document).
3) If you paste multiple links in one message, the bot only processes the first supported link.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token.

Optional tuning
1) RATE_LIMIT_X_PER_MIN (default 6)
2) RATE_LIMIT_TIKTOK_PER_MIN (default 6)
3) HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS (default 20000)
4) HTTP_RETRIES (default 2)
5) HTTP_MAX_REDIRECTS (default 5)
6) MAX_MEDIA_BYTES (default 47185920)

Troubleshooting
1) If you see 409 Conflict in logs, two bot instances are polling at once. The bot will back off and retry.
2) If downloads fail often for one platform, that platform may have changed its public page format. Check logs for the failing URL and HTTP status.

Usage
Paste a public TikTok or X link into chat. The bot will fetch the media and send it back.

Commands

2) /help

Notes
1) Public content only.
2) Telegram file limits apply.

More details are in DOCS.md.

// This is injected at generation-time so the bot can describe itself in plain chat.
export const BOT_PROMPT = "Build a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that lets users download and receive public media from TikTok and X by pasting a link in chat.

Core behavior:
- Implement /start and /help.
  - /start: brief welcome + examples of TikTok and X URLs users can paste.
  - /help: supported sources (TikTok, X), that ONLY public media works, typical failure reasons, and privacy note.
- For any non-command message that contains a URL, detect and extract the first supported TikTok or X status/video link (also handle multiple URLs by processing the first and telling user if others were ignored).
- Respond immediately with a status message (e.g., “Fetching media…”) and then update/replace it as the process continues (fetching → downloading → uploading).
- Download the best available public media and send it back into the same chat.
  - If a single video: send as video when possible; if Telegram rejects, fall back to sending as document.
  - If images (carousel): send as media group (album) where possible; if media group fails, send sequentially with short captions.
  - Always include a short caption with the original source link.
- If the link is private/locked/age-restricted/geo-blocked/deleted or requires login, reply with a clear “Public content only” error.
- If the link is unsupported or malformed, reply with a helpful message and examples.

  - src/services/x.js: given an X URL, resolve redirects and extract media URLs for public tweets/posts.
  - src/services/downloader.js: orchestrates detection, chooses correct provider, downloads media to a temp location or streams, returns a normalized result object.

  - bounded timeouts, limited retries, and a safe user-agent header";

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Media Downloader Bot (TikTok + X) using Node.js (ESM) and grammY

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that lets users paste public TikTok or X (Twitter) links and receive the downloadable media back in chat. The bot validates links, resolves direct media URLs via platform-specific extractors, downloads media to temporary storage, then sends it to the user as video/photo/document depending on Telegram constraints.

The system is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence (optional but recommended for rate limits, jobs, and observability). It includes robust error handling, anti-abuse measures, deduplication, a lightweight job pipeline within the process, and operational visibility (logs + basic metrics events).

Product goals:
Primary user goal: paste a public TikTok or X link and receive the media file (video/photo) in the same chat quickly and reliably.

Core product requirements:
1) Accept links in private chats and groups.
2) Support only public content. If the media is private, geo-restricted, removed, or requires authentication, the bot must explain it cannot process it.
3) Provide a simple UX: user pastes a link, bot replies with progress and then the media.
4) Be resilient to platform changes and rate limits by isolating extraction logic, adding retries/backoff, and providing graceful degradation.
5) Prevent abuse: user-level rate limiting, size limits, and basic anti-spam controls.

Non-goals (initially):
1) Downloading private media via login/cookies.
2) Complex editing/transcoding.
3) Long-term hosting of downloaded content for reuse beyond short-lived caching.

Success metrics:
1) Median time from link to delivered media.
2) Extraction success rate per platform.
3) Download/send failure rate (Telegram upload errors, file too large, timeouts).
4) Abuse incidents prevented by rate limiting.

High-level architecture:
System overview (single-process design):
1) grammY update handler receives messages and commands.
2) Link classifier identifies TikTok/X URLs and normalizes them.
3) Job manager creates an in-process download job with status tracking.
4) Extractor module resolves the public page into one or more media candidates (video variants, images, audio if applicable).
5) Downloader fetches the selected candidate to temporary storage (filesystem storage) with streaming and size guards.
6) Sender uploads to Telegram using the correct method (sendVideo/sendPhoto/sendDocument), choosing the best format/quality that fits limits.
7) Cleanup removes temporary files after send completion.

Component boundaries:
1) Bot API layer: grammY middleware for authentication checks, rate limits, and routing.
2) Domain layer: URL normalization, platform routing, job orchestration.
3) Platform adapters: TikTok adapter and X adapter. Each adapter exposes: validate(url), resolve(url) -> media candidates + metadata.
4) Media pipeline: downloader, file inspector (detect MIME/duration/size), Telegram sender, cleanup.
5) Persistence: MongoDB collections for users, jobs, and audit events (optional but recommended). In-memory maps can be used for minimal MVP, but production readiness benefits from MongoDB to survive restarts and support observability.

Runtime model:
1) Concurrency is limited with an internal queue/semaphore so the single process does not overwhelm CPU/network.
2) Each job has a state machine: queued -> resolving -> downloading -> sending -> done/failed.
3) A watchdog periodically scans for stuck jobs and marks them failed with a user-friendly message if they exceed time budgets.

External dependencies:
1) Telegram Bot API (via grammY).
2) Public web access to TikTok/X pages and media URLs.
3) MongoDB (already integrated) for persistence.
4) Temporary file storage via local filesystem storage (or object storage if later needed), but keep initial design as simple filesystem storage.

Operational considerations:
1) Structured logging for every job, including platform, timing, and failure reason.
2) Minimal health endpoint (HTTP) is optional; if present, it should only report process and MongoDB connectivity. Keep deployment generic as a Node.js server in the cloud.

Data model:
Recommended MongoDB collections (minimum production-ready):

1) users
Fields:
1) telegramUserId (number/string)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role (user/admin)
4) rateLimitState (rolling counters, lastResetAt) or store derived usage events elsewhere
5) settings (optional): preferred quality, caption on/off

2) jobs
Fields:
1) jobId (string)
2) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
3) platform (tiktok/x)
4) sourceUrlOriginal, sourceUrlNormalized
5) status (queued/resolving/downloading/sending/done/failed)
6) media
   1) type (video/photo/gallery)
   2) title/author (best-effort)
   3) candidates (array of { url, qualityLabel, mimeType, approxSize })
   4) selectedCandidate
7) file
   1) tempPath (filesystem storage path)
   2) sizeBytes
   3) mimeType
   4) checksum (optional)
8) telemetry
   1) timings: resolveMs, downloadMs, sendMs, totalMs
   2) errorCode, errorMessageSafe
9) createdAt, updatedAt, expiresAt (for cleanup policies)

3) events (optional but useful)
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) type (jobcreated, resolvefailed, ratelimited, sendfailed, etc.)
3) jobId, telegramUserId
4) payload (small, sanitized)

In-memory structures (still needed even with MongoDB):
1) Active job map: jobId -> runtime state (abort controller, progress, file stream handles).
2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.

Key flows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot interactions.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for users/jobs/events and to survive restarts.
2) In-memory maps for active jobs, locks, and short-lived caches.

Storage:
1) Simple filesystem storage for temporary downloaded media files.
2) Optional: object storage for temporary files if the filesystem is ephemeral, but keep the logic abstracted so it can be swapped without changing core flows.

Networking:
1) HTTP client for fetching public web pages and media URLs (choose one consistent client; keep timeouts/retries centralized).

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single process.
2) Configuration via environment variables (including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN).

Observability:
1) Structured logs (JSON-style) written to stdout.
2) Basic in-process metrics counters (jobs created/succeeded/failed, per-platform failures) emitted as log events.
3) Optional lightweight health check endpoint for uptime monitoring.

No additional databases or infrastructure are introduced beyond MongoDB and the single Node.js process constraints.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Concurrency limits: cap global concurrent downloads and per-platform concurrent resolves to prevent memory/network exhaustion.
2) Timeouts: strict timeouts for resolve, download, and send phases.
3) Retries with backoff: limited retries for transient network errors; no infinite retries.
4) Fallbacks: alternate media candidates when the preferred one fails.
5) Restart safety: store job status in MongoDB so you can diagnose failures after restarts; on startup, mark in-flight jobs as failed with a retriable message.

Performance:
1) Stream downloads to disk; avoid buffering large files in memory.
2) Avoid unnecessary re-resolves by caching normalized URL metadata briefly.
3) Use a single progress message edit rather than multiple messages.

Security:
1) URL allowlist and strict validation to prevent SSRF-like behavior.
2) Limit maximum download size and reject overly large media early when possible.
3) Sanitize filenames and never trust remote headers blindly.
4) Rate limiting per user and per chat to reduce abuse.
5) Admin controls for blocking users/chats.

Privacy:
1) Store only the minimum user data needed (Telegram IDs and timestamps).
2) Do not store downloaded media long term.
3) Provide a /privacy command explaining what data is stored and for how long.

Maintainability:
1) Platform adapters isolated behind a common interface so TikTok/X changes are localized.
2) Central error taxonomy (e.g., PRIVATECONTENT, NOTFOUND, RATELIMITEDUPSTREAM, TELEGRAMFILETOOLARGE) for consistent user messaging and metrics.
3) Configuration via environment variables (limits, timeouts, feature flags).

User experience:
1) Clear status updates and actionable errors.
2) If unsupported link: respond with supported platforms and examples.
3) If multiple links: either process the first or ask the user to send one at a time (configurable).

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it by sending a TikTok or X link. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show usage instructions, supported links, and common troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [General]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job logs) and retention/cleanup behavior. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /limits — Show current rate limits and maximum supported file sizes/timeouts. (usage: /limits) [General]
• /status — Show bot health summary and queue length (admin-only). (usage: /status) [Admin]
• /block — Block a user from using the bot (admin-only). (usage: /block <telegramUserId>) [Admin]
• /unblock — Unblock a previously blocked user (admin-only). (usage: /unblock <telegramUserId>) [Admin]

DOCS.md.content:
This bot downloads public media from TikTok and X links pasted into Telegram and sends the media back into the same chat.

Requirements
Node.js >= 18.

Setup
1) Create a Telegram bot with BotFather and get your token.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.
3) Install dependencies:
   npm run build
4) Run the bot:
   npm start

Development
1) npm run dev

Note about environment loading
1) .env is loaded by Node via --env-file=.env (see package.json scripts).
2) dotenv is still installed and can be used internally by the codebase without breaking anything, but it is not required for local dev when you use the provided scripts.

Commands
1) /start
Sends a welcome message and examples of TikTok and X URLs you can paste.

2) /help
Explains supported sources (TikTok and X), public-only limitations, common failure reasons, and privacy notes.

How it works
1) Paste a public TikTok or X link into chat.
2) The bot replies with a status message and updates it as it works (resolving, downloading, uploading).
3) The bot sends back the best available video or images.

Limitations
1) Public content only. Private, locked, age-restricted, geo-blocked, deleted content, or content requiring login cannot be downloaded.
2) Telegram upload limits apply. If a file is too large, Telegram may reject it. The bot will try fallbacks (video to document).
3) If you paste multiple links in one message, the bot only processes the first supported link.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token.

Optional tuning
1) RATE_LIMIT_X_PER_MIN (default 6)
2) RATE_LIMIT_TIKTOK_PER_MIN (default 6)
3) HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS (default 20000)
4) HTTP_RETRIES (default 2)
5) HTTP_MAX_REDIRECTS (default 5)
6) MAX_MEDIA_BYTES (default 47185920)

Troubleshooting
1) If you see 409 Conflict in logs, two bot instances are polling at once. The bot will back off and retry.
2) If downloads fail often for one platform, that platform may have changed its public page format. Check logs for the failing URL and HTTP status.

// This is injected at generation-time so the bot can describe itself in plain chat.
export const BOT_PROMPT = "Build a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that lets users download and receive public media from TikTok and X by pasting a link in chat.

Core behavior:
- Implement /start and /help.
  - /start: brief welcome + examples of TikTok and X URLs users can paste.
  - /help: supported sources (TikTok, X), that ONLY public media works, typical failure reasons, and privacy note.
- For any non-command message that contains a URL, detect and extract the first supported TikTok or X status/video link (also handle multiple URLs by processing the first and telling user if others were ignored).
- Respond immediately with a status message (e.g., “Fetching media…”) and then update/replace it as the process continues (fetching → downloading → uploading).
- Download the best available public media and send it back into the same chat.
  - If a single video: send as video when possible; if Telegram rejects, fall back to sending as document.
  - If images (carousel): send as media group (album) where possible; if media group fails, send sequentially with short captions.
  - Always include a short caption with the original source link.
- If the link is private/locked/age-restricted/geo-blocked/deleted or requires login, reply with a clear “Public content only” error.
- If the link is unsupported or malformed, reply with a helpful message and examples.

Downloader implementation requirements (no low-level code in this spec, but the generator must implement them):
- Create service modules:
  - src/services/tiktok.js: given a TikTok URL, resolve redirects, extract media download URLs for public content.
  - src/services/x.js: given an X URL, resolve redirects and extract media URLs for public tweets/posts.
  - src/services/downloader.js: orchestrates detection, chooses correct provider, downloads media to a temp location or streams, returns a normalized result object.
- Use a shared HTTP helper (src/lib/http.js) with:
  - bounded timeouts, limited retries, and a safe user-agent header";

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Media Downloader Bot (TikTok + X) using Node.js (ESM) and grammY

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that lets users paste public TikTok or X (Twitter) links and receive the downloadable media back in chat. The bot validates links, resolves direct media URLs via platform-specific extractors, downloads media to temporary storage, then sends it to the user as video/photo/document depending on Telegram constraints.

The system is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence (optional but recommended for rate limits, jobs, and observability). It includes robust error handling, anti-abuse measures, deduplication, a lightweight job pipeline within the process, and operational visibility (logs + basic metrics events).

Product goals:
Primary user goal: paste a public TikTok or X link and receive the media file (video/photo) in the same chat quickly and reliably.

Core product requirements:
1) Accept links in private chats and groups.
2) Support only public content. If the media is private, geo-restricted, removed, or requires authentication, the bot must explain it cannot process it.
3) Provide a simple UX: user pastes a link, bot replies with progress and then the media.
4) Be resilient to platform changes and rate limits by isolating extraction logic, adding retries/backoff, and providing graceful degradation.
5) Prevent abuse: user-level rate limiting, size limits, and basic anti-spam controls.

Non-goals (initially):
1) Downloading private media via login/cookies.
2) Complex editing/transcoding.
3) Long-term hosting of downloaded content for reuse beyond short-lived caching.

Success metrics:
1) Median time from link to delivered media.
2) Extraction success rate per platform.
3) Download/send failure rate (Telegram upload errors, file too large, timeouts).
4) Abuse incidents prevented by rate limiting.

High-level architecture:
System overview (single-process design):
1) grammY update handler receives messages and commands.
2) Link classifier identifies TikTok/X URLs and normalizes them.
3) Job manager creates an in-process download job with status tracking.
4) Extractor module resolves the public page into one or more media candidates (video variants, images, audio if applicable).
5) Downloader fetches the selected candidate to temporary storage (filesystem storage) with streaming and size guards.
6) Sender uploads to Telegram using the correct method (sendVideo/sendPhoto/sendDocument), choosing the best format/quality that fits limits.
7) Cleanup removes temporary files after send completion.

Component boundaries:
1) Bot API layer: grammY middleware for authentication checks, rate limits, and routing.
2) Domain layer: URL normalization, platform routing, job orchestration.
3) Platform adapters: TikTok adapter and X adapter. Each adapter exposes: validate(url), resolve(url) -> media candidates + metadata.
4) Media pipeline: downloader, file inspector (detect MIME/duration/size), Telegram sender, cleanup.
5) Persistence: MongoDB collections for users, jobs, and audit events (optional but recommended). In-memory maps can be used for minimal MVP, but production readiness benefits from MongoDB to survive restarts and support observability.

Runtime model:
1) Concurrency is limited with an internal queue/semaphore so the single process does not overwhelm CPU/network.
2) Each job has a state machine: queued -> resolving -> downloading -> sending -> done/failed.
3) A watchdog periodically scans for stuck jobs and marks them failed with a user-friendly message if they exceed time budgets.

External dependencies:
1) Telegram Bot API (via grammY).
2) Public web access to TikTok/X pages and media URLs.
3) MongoDB (already integrated) for persistence.
4) Temporary file storage via local filesystem storage (or object storage if later needed), but keep initial design as simple filesystem storage.

Operational considerations:
1) Structured logging for every job, including platform, timing, and failure reason.
2) Minimal health endpoint (HTTP) is optional; if present, it should only report process and MongoDB connectivity. Keep deployment generic as a Node.js server in the cloud.

Data model:
Recommended MongoDB collections (minimum production-ready):

1) users
Fields:
1) telegramUserId (number/string)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role (user/admin)
4) rateLimitState (rolling counters, lastResetAt) or store derived usage events elsewhere
5) settings (optional): preferred quality, caption on/off

2) jobs
Fields:
1) jobId (string)
2) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
3) platform (tiktok/x)
4) sourceUrlOriginal, sourceUrlNormalized
5) status (queued/resolving/downloading/sending/done/failed)
6) media
   1) type (video/photo/gallery)
   2) title/author (best-effort)
   3) candidates (array of { url, qualityLabel, mimeType, approxSize })
   4) selectedCandidate
7) file
   1) tempPath (filesystem storage path)
   2) sizeBytes
   3) mimeType
   4) checksum (optional)
8) telemetry
   1) timings: resolveMs, downloadMs, sendMs, totalMs
   2) errorCode, errorMessageSafe
9) createdAt, updatedAt, expiresAt (for cleanup policies)

3) events (optional but useful)
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) type (jobcreated, resolvefailed, ratelimited, sendfailed, etc.)
3) jobId, telegramUserId
4) payload (small, sanitized)

In-memory structures (still needed even with MongoDB):
1) Active job map: jobId -> runtime state (abort controller, progress, file stream handles).
2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.

Key flows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot interactions.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for users/jobs/events and to survive restarts.
2) In-memory maps for active jobs, locks, and short-lived caches.

Storage:
1) Simple filesystem storage for temporary downloaded media files.
2) Optional: object storage for temporary files if the filesystem is ephemeral, but keep the logic abstracted so it can be swapped without changing core flows.

Networking:
1) HTTP client for fetching public web pages and media URLs (choose one consistent client; keep timeouts/retries centralized).

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single process.
2) Configuration via environment variables (including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN).

Observability:
1) Structured logs (JSON-style) written to stdout.
2) Basic in-process metrics counters (jobs created/succeeded/failed, per-platform failures) emitted as log events.
3) Optional lightweight health check endpoint for uptime monitoring.

No additional databases or infrastructure are introduced beyond MongoDB and the single Node.js process constraints.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Concurrency limits: cap global concurrent downloads and per-platform concurrent resolves to prevent memory/network exhaustion.
2) Timeouts: strict timeouts for resolve, download, and send phases.
3) Retries with backoff: limited retries for transient network errors; no infinite retries.
4) Fallbacks: alternate media candidates when the preferred one fails.
5) Restart safety: store job status in MongoDB so you can diagnose failures after restarts; on startup, mark in-flight jobs as failed with a retriable message.

Performance:
1) Stream downloads to disk; avoid buffering large files in memory.
2) Avoid unnecessary re-resolves by caching normalized URL metadata briefly.
3) Use a single progress message edit rather than multiple messages.

Security:
1) URL allowlist and strict validation to prevent SSRF-like behavior.
2) Limit maximum download size and reject overly large media early when possible.
3) Sanitize filenames and never trust remote headers blindly.
4) Rate limiting per user and per chat to reduce abuse.
5) Admin controls for blocking users/chats.

Privacy:
1) Store only the minimum user data needed (Telegram IDs and timestamps).
2) Do not store downloaded media long term.
3) Provide a /privacy command explaining what data is stored and for how long.

Maintainability:
1) Platform adapters isolated behind a common interface so TikTok/X changes are localized.
2) Central error taxonomy (e.g., PRIVATECONTENT, NOTFOUND, RATELIMITEDUPSTREAM, TELEGRAMFILETOOLARGE) for consistent user messaging and metrics.
3) Configuration via environment variables (limits, timeouts, feature flags).

User experience:
1) Clear status updates and actionable errors.
2) If unsupported link: respond with supported platforms and examples.
3) If multiple links: either process the first or ask the user to send one at a time (configurable).

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it by sending a TikTok or X link. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show usage instructions, supported links, and common troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [General]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job logs) and retention/cleanup behavior. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /limits — Show current rate limits and maximum supported file sizes/timeouts. (usage: /limits) [General]
• /status — Show bot health summary and queue length (admin-only). (usage: /status) [Admin]
• /block — Block a user from using the bot (admin-only). (usage: /block <telegramUserId>) [Admin]
• /unblock — Unblock a previously blocked user (admin-only). (usage: /unblock <telegramUserId>) [Admin]

2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot interactions.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for users/jobs/events and to survive restarts.
2) In-memory maps for active jobs, locks, and short-lived caches.

Storage:
1) Simple filesystem storage for temporary downloaded media files.
2) Optional: object storage for temporary files if the filesystem is ephemeral, but keep the logic abstracted so it can be swapped without changing core flows.

Networking:
1) HTTP client for fetching public web pages and media URLs (choose one consistent client; keep timeouts/retries centralized).

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single process.
2) Configuration via environment variables (including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN).

Observability:
1) Structured logs (JSON-style) written to stdout.
2) Basic in-process metrics counters (jobs created/succeeded/failed, per-platform failures) emitted as log events.
3) Optional lightweight health check endpoint for uptime monitoring.

No additional databases or infrastructure are introduced beyond MongoDB and the single Node.js process constraints.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability:
1) Concurrency limits: cap global concurrent downloads and per-platform concurrent resolves to prevent memory/network exhaustion.
2) Timeouts: strict timeouts for resolve, download, and send phases.
3) Retries with backoff: limited retries for transient network errors; no infinite retries.
4) Fallbacks: alternate media candidates when the preferred one fails.
5) Restart safety: store job status in MongoDB so you can diagnose failures after restarts; on startup, mark in-flight jobs as failed with a retriable message.

Performance:
1) Stream downloads to disk; avoid buffering large files in memory.
2) Avoid unnecessary re-resolves by caching normalized URL metadata briefly.
3) Use a single progress message edit rather than multiple messages.

Security:
1) URL allowlist and strict validation to prevent SSRF-like behavior.
2) Limit maximum download size and reject overly large media early when possible.
3) Sanitize filenames and never trust remote headers blindly.
4) Rate limiting per user and per chat to reduce abuse.
5) Admin controls for blocking users/chats.

Privacy:
1) Store only the minimum user data needed (Telegram IDs and timestamps).
2) Do not store downloaded media long term.
3) Provide a /privacy command explaining what data is stored and for how long.

Maintainability:
1) Platform adapters isolated behind a common interface so TikTok/X changes are localized.
2) Central error taxonomy (e.g., PRIVATECONTENT, NOTFOUND, RATELIMITEDUPSTREAM, TELEGRAMFILETOOLARGE) for consistent user messaging and metrics.
3) Configuration via environment variables (limits, timeouts, feature flags).

User experience:
1) Clear status updates and actionable errors.
2) If unsupported link: respond with supported platforms and examples.
3) If multiple links: either process the first or ask the user to send one at a time (configurable).

DOCS.md.content:
Bot not responding troubleshooting
1) Check TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set in your environment.
2) If you see 409 Conflict in logs, another instance may be running or a webhook is set. Stop other instances, or clear webhook, then restart.
3) This bot prefers polling (getUpdates). If you set TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_URL without a working PORT listener, it will log a warning and still use polling.
4) Check logs for [polling] starting and [polling] alive lines. If missing, the process may not be starting.
5) If MongoDB connection fails, the bot should still respond to /start and /help; check [db] connect failed logs for details.

README.md.content:
Bot not responding troubleshooting
1) Verify TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
2) Look for [tg] bot.init ok and [polling] starting in logs.
3) If you see 409 Conflict, you likely have two deployments running at once or a webhook configured. The bot will back off and retry automatically.
4) If you configured TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_URL but did not expose PORT, the bot will fall back to polling.
5) Use /health (admin-only) to see update mode, DB connectivity, and last error summary.

// This is injected at generation-time so the bot can describe itself in plain chat.
export const BOT_PROMPT = "Build a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that lets users download and receive public media from TikTok and X by pasting a link in chat.

Core behavior:
- Implement /start and /help.
  - /start: brief welcome + examples of TikTok and X URLs users can paste.
  - /help: supported sources (TikTok, X), that ONLY public media works, typical failure reasons, and privacy note.
- For any non-command message that contains a URL, detect and extract the first supported TikTok or X status/video link (also handle multiple URLs by processing the first and telling user if others were ignored).
- Respond immediately with a status message (e.g., “Fetching media…”) and then update/replace it as the process continues (fetching → downloading → uploading).
- Download the best available public media and send it back into the same chat.
  - If a single video: send as video when possible; if Telegram rejects, fall back to sending as document.
  - If images (carousel): send as media group (album) where possible; if media group fails, send sequentially with short captions.
  - Always include a short caption with the original source link.
- If the link is private/locked/age-restricted/geo-blocked/deleted or requires login, reply with a clear “Public content only” error.
- If the link is unsupported or malformed, reply with a helpful message and examples.

Downloader implementation requirements (no low-level code in this spec, but the generator must implement them):
- Create service modules:
  - src/services/tiktok.js: given a TikTok URL, resolve redirects, extract media download URLs for public content.
  - src/services/x.js: given an X URL, resolve redirects and extract media URLs for public tweets/posts.
  - src/services/downloader.js: orchestrates detection, chooses correct provider, downloads media to a temp location or streams, returns a normalized result object.
- Use a shared HTTP helper (src/lib/http.js) with:
  - bounded timeouts, limited retries, and a safe user-agent header";

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Media Downloader Bot (TikTok + X) using Node.js (ESM) and grammY

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that lets users paste public TikTok or X (Twitter) links and receive the downloadable media back in chat. The bot validates links, resolves direct media URLs via platform-specific extractors, downloads media to temporary storage, then sends it to the user as video/photo/document depending on Telegram constraints.

The system is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence (optional but recommended for rate limits, jobs, and observability). It includes robust error handling, anti-abuse measures, deduplication, a lightweight job pipeline within the process, and operational visibility (logs + basic metrics events).

Product goals:
Primary user goal: paste a public TikTok or X link and receive the media file (video/photo) in the same chat quickly and reliably.

Core product requirements:
1) Accept links in private chats and groups.
2) Support only public content. If the media is private, geo-restricted, removed, or requires authentication, the bot must explain it cannot process it.
3) Provide a simple UX: user pastes a link, bot replies with progress and then the media.
4) Be resilient to platform changes and rate limits by isolating extraction logic, adding retries/backoff, and providing graceful degradation.
5) Prevent abuse: user-level rate limiting, size limits, and basic anti-spam controls.

Non-goals (initially):
1) Downloading private media via login/cookies.
2) Complex editing/transcoding.
3) Long-term hosting of downloaded content for reuse beyond short-lived caching.

Success metrics:
1) Median time from link to delivered media.
2) Extraction success rate per platform.
3) Download/send failure rate (Telegram upload errors, file too large, timeouts).
4) Abuse incidents prevented by rate limiting.

High-level architecture:
System overview (single-process design):
1) grammY update handler receives messages and commands.
2) Link classifier identifies TikTok/X URLs and normalizes them.
3) Job manager creates an in-process download job with status tracking.
4) Extractor module resolves the public page into one or more media candidates (video variants, images, audio if applicable).
5) Downloader fetches the selected candidate to temporary storage (filesystem storage) with streaming and size guards.
6) Sender uploads to Telegram using the correct method (sendVideo/sendPhoto/sendDocument), choosing the best format/quality that fits limits.
7) Cleanup removes temporary files after send completion.

Component boundaries:
1) Bot API layer: grammY middleware for authentication checks, rate limits, and routing.
2) Domain layer: URL normalization, platform routing, job orchestration.
3) Platform adapters: TikTok adapter and X adapter. Each adapter exposes: validate(url), resolve(url) -> media candidates + metadata.
4) Media pipeline: downloader, file inspector (detect MIME/duration/size), Telegram sender, cleanup.
5) Persistence: MongoDB collections for users, jobs, and audit events (optional but recommended). In-memory maps can be used for minimal MVP, but production readiness benefits from MongoDB to survive restarts and support observability.

Runtime model:
1) Concurrency is limited with an internal queue/semaphore so the single process does not overwhelm CPU/network.
2) Each job has a state machine: queued -> resolving -> downloading -> sending -> done/failed.
3) A watchdog periodically scans for stuck jobs and marks them failed with a user-friendly message if they exceed time budgets.

External dependencies:
1) Telegram Bot API (via grammY).
2) Public web access to TikTok/X pages and media URLs.
3) MongoDB (already integrated) for persistence.
4) Temporary file storage via local filesystem storage (or object storage if later needed), but keep initial design as simple filesystem storage.

Operational considerations:
1) Structured logging for every job, including platform, timing, and failure reason.
2) Minimal health endpoint (HTTP) is optional; if present, it should only report process and MongoDB connectivity. Keep deployment generic as a Node.js server in the cloud.

Data model:
Recommended MongoDB collections (minimum production-ready):

1) users
Fields:
1) telegramUserId (number/string)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role (user/admin)
4) rateLimitState (rolling counters, lastResetAt) or store derived usage events elsewhere
5) settings (optional): preferred quality, caption on/off

2) jobs
Fields:
1) jobId (string)
2) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
3) platform (tiktok/x)
4) sourceUrlOriginal, sourceUrlNormalized
5) status (queued/resolving/downloading/sending/done/failed)
6) media
   1) type (video/photo/gallery)
   2) title/author (best-effort)
   3) candidates (array of { url, qualityLabel, mimeType, approxSize })
   4) selectedCandidate
7) file
   1) tempPath (filesystem storage path)
   2) sizeBytes
   3) mimeType
   4) checksum (optional)
8) telemetry
   1) timings: resolveMs, downloadMs, sendMs, totalMs
   2) errorCode, errorMessageSafe
9) createdAt, updatedAt, expiresAt (for cleanup policies)

3) events (optional but useful)
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) type (jobcreated, resolvefailed, ratelimited, sendfailed, etc.)
3) jobId, telegramUserId
4) payload (small, sanitized)

In-memory structures (still needed even with MongoDB):
1) Active job map: jobId -> runtime state (abort controller, progress, file stream handles).
2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.

Key flows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot interactions.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for users/jobs/events and to survive restarts.
2) In-memory maps for active jobs, locks, and short-lived caches.

Storage:
1) Simple filesystem storage for temporary downloaded media files.
2) Optional: object storage for temporary files if the filesystem is ephemeral, but keep the logic abstracted so it can be swapped without changing core flows.

Networking:
1) HTTP client for fetching public web pages and media URLs (choose one consistent client; keep timeouts/retries centralized).

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single process.
2) Configuration via environment variables (including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN).

Observability:
1) Structured logs (JSON-style) written to stdout.
2) Basic in-process metrics counters (jobs created/succeeded/failed, per-platform failures) emitted as log events.
3) Optional lightweight health check endpoint for uptime monitoring.

No additional databases or infrastructure are introduced beyond MongoDB and the single Node.js process constraints.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Concurrency limits: cap global concurrent downloads and per-platform concurrent resolves to prevent memory/network exhaustion.
2) Timeouts: strict timeouts for resolve, download, and send phases.
3) Retries with backoff: limited retries for transient network errors; no infinite retries.
4) Fallbacks: alternate media candidates when the preferred one fails.
5) Restart safety: store job status in MongoDB so you can diagnose failures after restarts; on startup, mark in-flight jobs as failed with a retriable message.

Performance:
1) Stream downloads to disk; avoid buffering large files in memory.
2) Avoid unnecessary re-resolves by caching normalized URL metadata briefly.
3) Use a single progress message edit rather than multiple messages.

Security:
1) URL allowlist and strict validation to prevent SSRF-like behavior.
2) Limit maximum download size and reject overly large media early when possible.
3) Sanitize filenames and never trust remote headers blindly.
4) Rate limiting per user and per chat to reduce abuse.
5) Admin controls for blocking users/chats.

Privacy:
1) Store only the minimum user data needed (Telegram IDs and timestamps).
2) Do not store downloaded media long term.
3) Provide a /privacy command explaining what data is stored and for how long.

Maintainability:
1) Platform adapters isolated behind a common interface so TikTok/X changes are localized.
2) Central error taxonomy (e.g., PRIVATECONTENT, NOTFOUND, RATELIMITEDUPSTREAM, TELEGRAMFILETOOLARGE) for consistent user messaging and metrics.
3) Configuration via environment variables (limits, timeouts, feature flags).

User experience:
1) Clear status updates and actionable errors.
2) If unsupported link: respond with supported platforms and examples.
3) If multiple links: either process the first or ask the user to send one at a time (configurable).

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it by sending a TikTok or X link. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show usage instructions, supported links, and common troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [General]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job logs) and retention/cleanup behavior. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /limits — Show current rate limits and maximum supported file sizes/timeouts. (usage: /limits) [General]
• /status — Show bot health summary and queue length (admin-only). (usage: /status) [Admin]
• /block — Block a user from using the bot (admin-only). (usage: /block <telegramUserId>) [Admin]
• /unblock — Unblock a previously blocked user (admin-only). (usage: /unblock <telegramUserId>) [Admin]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary user goal: paste a public TikTok or X link and receive the media file (video/photo) in the same chat quickly and reliably.

Core product requirements:
1) Accept links in private chats and groups.
2) Support only public content. If the media is private, geo-restricted, removed, or requires authentication, the bot must explain it cannot process it.
3) Provide a simple UX: user pastes a link, bot replies with progress and then the media.
4) Be resilient to platform changes and rate limits by isolating extraction logic, adding retries/backoff, and providing graceful degradation.
5) Prevent abuse: user-level rate limiting, size limits, and basic anti-spam controls.

Non-goals (initially):
1) Downloading private media via login/cookies.
2) Complex editing/transcoding.
3) Long-term hosting of downloaded content for reuse beyond short-lived caching.

Success metrics:
1) Median time from link to delivered media.
2) Extraction success rate per platform.
3) Download/send failure rate (Telegram upload errors, file too large, timeouts).
4) Abuse incidents prevented by rate limiting.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
System overview (single-process design):
1) grammY update handler receives messages and commands.
2) Link classifier identifies TikTok/X URLs and normalizes them.
3) Job manager creates an in-process download job with status tracking.
4) Extractor module resolves the public page into one or more media candidates (video variants, images, audio if applicable).
5) Downloader fetches the selected candidate to temporary storage (filesystem storage) with streaming and size guards.
6) Sender uploads to Telegram using the correct method (sendVideo/sendPhoto/sendDocument), choosing the best format/quality that fits limits.
7) Cleanup removes temporary files after send completion.

Component boundaries:
1) Bot API layer: grammY middleware for authentication checks, rate limits, and routing.
2) Domain layer: URL normalization, platform routing, job orchestration.
3) Platform adapters: TikTok adapter and X adapter. Each adapter exposes: validate(url), resolve(url) -> media candidates + metadata.
4) Media pipeline: downloader, file inspector (detect MIME/duration/size), Telegram sender, cleanup.
5) Persistence: MongoDB collections for users, jobs, and audit events (optional but recommended). In-memory maps can be used for minimal MVP, but production readiness benefits from MongoDB to survive restarts and support observability.

Runtime model:
1) Concurrency is limited with an internal queue/semaphore so the single process does not overwhelm CPU/network.
2) Each job has a state machine: queued -> resolving -> downloading -> sending -> done/failed.
3) A watchdog periodically scans for stuck jobs and marks them failed with a user-friendly message if they exceed time budgets.

External dependencies:
1) Telegram Bot API (via grammY).
2) Public web access to TikTok/X pages and media URLs.
3) MongoDB (already integrated) for persistence.
4) Temporary file storage via local filesystem storage (or object storage if later needed), but keep initial design as simple filesystem storage.

Operational considerations:
1) Structured logging for every job, including platform, timing, and failure reason.
2) Minimal health endpoint (HTTP) is optional; if present, it should only report process and MongoDB connectivity. Keep deployment generic as a Node.js server in the cloud.
planSections.dataModel:
Recommended MongoDB collections (minimum production-ready):

1) users
Fields:
1) telegramUserId (number/string)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role (user/admin)
4) rateLimitState (rolling counters, lastResetAt) or store derived usage events elsewhere
5) settings (optional): preferred quality, caption on/off

2) jobs
Fields:
1) jobId (string)
2) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
3) platform (tiktok/x)
4) sourceUrlOriginal, sourceUrlNormalized
5) status (queued/resolving/downloading/sending/done/failed)
6) media
   1) type (video/photo/gallery)
   2) title/author (best-effort)
   3) candidates (array of { url, qualityLabel, mimeType, approxSize })
   4) selectedCandidate
7) file
   1) tempPath (filesystem storage path)
   2) sizeBytes
   3) mimeType
   4) checksum (optional)
8) telemetry
   1) timings: resolveMs, downloadMs, sendMs, totalMs
   2) errorCode, errorMessageSafe
9) createdAt, updatedAt, expiresAt (for cleanup policies)

3) events (optional but useful)
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) type (jobcreated, resolvefailed, ratelimited, sendfailed, etc.)
3) jobId, telegramUserId
4) payload (small, sanitized)

In-memory structures (still needed even with MongoDB):
1) Active job map: jobId -> runtime state (abort controller, progress, file stream handles).
2) Concurrency limiter: per-platform and global.
3) Short-lived dedupe cache: normalizedUrl -> lastJobId/result metadata to avoid repeated downloads in a short window.

Data retention:
1) Keep jobs for a short period (for debugging and user support), then purge (e.g., via a periodic cleanup task).
2) Temporary media files must be deleted quickly after sending (or after a short TTL if send retries are needed).
3) Do not store full media long-term unless explicitly required; the bot should be a downloader/forwarder, not a hosting service.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Basic download flow (single link)
1) User sends a message containing a URL.
2) Bot extracts URLs from text and picks the first supported one (or supports multiple sequentially with user confirmation).
3) Bot normalizes URL (remove tracking params, expand short links if necessary) and identifies platform.
4) Bot checks rate limits (per-user and per-chat). If exceeded, reply with a friendly cooldown message.
5) Bot creates a job record (MongoDB) and replies with a status message like “Processing link…”.
6) Resolver (platform adapter) fetches the public page and extracts candidate media URLs and metadata.
7) Bot selects a candidate based on heuristics:
   1) Prefer direct MP4 for video.
   2) Prefer a size that fits Telegram constraints.
   3) If multiple photos (gallery), plan to send as album or multiple messages (within Telegram limits).
8) Downloader streams media to filesystem storage with:
   1) Max size guard.
   2) Timeout.
   3) Retry with backoff for transient failures.
9) Sender uploads to Telegram:
   1) If video: sendVideo when possible, else sendDocument.
   2) If photo: sendPhoto (or album if multiple).
   3) Add caption with source and optional metadata.
10) Bot edits the status message to “Done” or deletes it.
11) Cleanup deletes temporary file and updates job status.

2) Failure handling flow
1) Resolver fails (private/removed/blocked): job status -> failed; reply with a clear explanation and suggest checking link privacy.
2) Download fails (network/403/size too large): attempt alternative candidate if available; otherwise fail with reason.
3) Telegram upload fails (file too large or API error): fallback to sendDocument or lower-quality candidate; if still failing, respond with an explanation.
4) If job exceeds time budget: abort download, mark failed, reply with “Timed out, try again later.”

3) Deduplication flow
1) If the same normalized URL is requested repeatedly within a short window:
   1) If prior job succeeded and media is still available in temp cache (rare, because we delete quickly), resend from cached file.
   2) Otherwise reuse the previously resolved candidate list (metadata cache) to skip page parsing when safe.

4) Admin/operations flow
1) Admin can check bot status: running, queue length, recent error rates.
2) Admin can temporarily block abusive users or chats.
3) Admin can adjust rate limits via environment configuration (and optionally persist settings in MongoDB).

5) Group chat behavior
1) If a group has privacy mode enabled, require explicit mention or command usage.
2) In groups, avoid noisy progress updates; prefer a single reply that gets edited.
3) Optionally restrict the bot to only respond when the message contains exactly one supported link to reduce accidental triggers.

6) Security and compliance flow
1) Only download from supported domains and validated URL patterns.
2) Do not execute arbitrary redirects to unknown hosts.
3) Sanitize all user-visible errors.
4) Avoid storing personal data beyond what is necessary for rate limiting and support.
